back_translation: Now Naaman, the commander of the army of the king of Aram, was a great man before his master and highly regarded, because through him the LORD had given victory to Aram. He was a mighty man of valor, but he was a leper.
The Arameans had gone out in raiding bands and had taken a young girl from the land of Israel, and she served Naaman’s wife.
She said to her mistress, “If only my master would go to the prophet who is in Samaria, he would cure him of his leprosy.”
So Naaman went and told his master what the girl from the land of Israel had said.
The king of Aram said, “Go now, and I will send a letter to the king of Israel.” So Naaman went, taking ten talents of silver, six thousand shekels of gold, and ten sets of clothing.
The letter he took to the king of Israel said, “With this letter I am sending you my servant Naaman, so that you may cure him of his leprosy.”

When the king of Israel read the letter, he tore his clothes and said, “Am I God, to kill and to make alive, that this man sends to me to cure a man of his leprosy? See how he is seeking a quarrel with me!”

When Elisha the man of God heard that the king of Israel had torn his clothes, he sent to the king, saying, “Why have you torn your clothes? Let the man come to me, and he will know that there is a prophet in Israel.”
So Naaman came with his horses and chariots and stood at the door of Elisha’s house.
Elisha sent a messenger out to him, saying, “Go and wash yourself seven times in the Jordan, and your flesh will be restored and you will be clean.”
But Naaman went away angry, saying, “I thought he would surely come out to me, stand and call on the name of the LORD his God, and wave his hand over the affected area to cure my leprosy.
Are not the Abanah and the Pharpar, the rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel? Could I not wash in them and be clean?” So he turned and went away in anger.
But Naaman’s servants came and said to him, “My father, if the prophet had told you to do some great thing, would you not have done it? How much more, then, when he tells you, ‘Wash and be clean’?”
So Naaman went down and dipped himself seven times in the Jordan, according to the word of the man of God, and his flesh was restored like the flesh of a little child, and he was clean.

Then Naaman and all his attendants returned to the man of God, stood before him, and said, “Now I know for sure that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel. So please accept a gift from your servant.”
But Elisha said, “As the LORD lives, before whom I stand, I will not accept it.” And though Naaman urged him to accept it, he refused.
Then Naaman said, “If not, please let your servant be given as much soil as a pair of mules can carry, for your servant will no longer offer burnt offering or sacrifice to any other god but the LORD.

In this matter may the LORD forgive your servant: when my master goes into the temple of Rimmon to worship there, leaning on my arm, and I bow in the temple of Rimmon—when I bow in the temple of Rimmon, may the LORD forgive your servant in this matter.”
Elisha said, “Go in peace.” But when Naaman had traveled a short distance from him,

Gehazi, the servant of Elisha the man of God, thought, “Look, my master has spared this Aramean Naaman by not accepting what he brought. As the LORD lives, I will run after him and take something from him.”
So Gehazi pursued Naaman. When Naaman saw him running after him, he got down from the chariot to meet him and asked, “Is everything all right?”

Gehazi said, “Everything is all right. My master has sent me to say, ‘Just now two young men from the sons of the prophets have come to me from the hill country of Ephraim; please give them a talent of silver and two sets of clothing.’”

Naaman said, “Please take two talents,” and he urged Gehazi to accept. He tied up two talents of silver in two bags with two sets of clothing and gave them to two of his servants, who carried them ahead of Gehazi.
When Gehazi came to the hill, he took the things from the servants and stored them in the house; then he sent the men away, and they departed.
When Gehazi went in and stood before his master, Elisha asked, “Gehazi, where have you been?” He said, “Your servant did not go anywhere.”

But Elisha said, “Did not my heart go with you when the man got down from his chariot to meet you? Is this a time to accept money, clothing, olive groves, vineyards, sheep, oxen, and male and female servants?
Therefore the leprosy of Naaman will cling to you and your descendants forever.” And Gehazi went out from his presence leprous, as white as snow.
